class_schedule.topic: The Landscape of Professional Development and Corporate Training
learning_objectives: Distinguish between professional development, corporate training, and continuing education
Identify the key roles of an instructional designer in training contexts
Analyze the business case for effective training programs
readings: Noe, R. - Employee Training and Development Ch. 1-2
ATD State of the Industry Report (current year)

lecture_notes.introduction: Professional development and corporate training represent one of the largest employment markets for educational technologists. This session maps the landscape and distinguishes training contexts from academic education.
key_concepts: Corporate training: compliance, skills, leadership, onboarding
Professional development: ongoing growth in a profession (teacher PD, medical CE)
Continuing education: formal credit for professional licensure
Training as performance improvement: not all performance problems need training
The business case: ROI, productivity, retention, compliance risk reduction
lecture_notes.demonstration: Training needs analysis scenario: a company reports high customer complaint rates. Conduct a rapid needs analysis to determine whether training is the appropriate intervention.

review.value: Training is appropriate for knowledge and skill gaps. Performance support (job aids, checklists, search tools) is more efficient for information recall tasks. Many organizations over-train and under-support.

U.S. organizations spend over $100 billion annually on training. Average spend per employee is approximately $1,300. The ed tech professional who can demonstrate ROI has a significant career advantage.

class_schedule.topic: Adult Learning Principles in Professional Contexts
learning_objectives: Apply Knowles' andragogy principles to training design
Design experiential learning activities for adult professionals
Address resistance to training and change management challenges
readings: Knowles, M. - The Adult Learner Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Adult learners bring rich experience, strong opinions, and limited patience for content that does not respect their time. Training that ignores adult learning principles will fail regardless of production quality.
key_concepts: Knowles' assumptions: self-directed, experience-based, relevance-driven, problem-oriented
Kolb's experiential learning cycle: experience, reflect, conceptualize, experiment
70-20-10 model: 70% on-the-job, 20% social, 10% formal training
Overcoming resistance: relevance, respect, immediate applicability
Just-in-time training vs. just-in-case training
lecture_notes.demonstration: Training redesign exercise: transform a compliance training module from a content-dump format into an experiential learning activity applying Kolb's cycle

review.value: Most workplace learning happens informally through experience (70%) and social interaction (20%). Formal training (10%) should focus on foundational knowledge and skill building that is then reinforced through the other 90%.
Adult learners need to know WHY they are learning something before engaging. Start every training session with a real-world scenario or problem that demonstrates why this matters to THEM.

learning_objectives: Compare synchronous, asynchronous, and blended training delivery models
Design microlearning campaigns for workplace training
Evaluate training platforms and authoring tools for corporate contexts
readings: Clark, R. & Mayer, R. - e-Learning and the Science of Instruction Ch. 1-3
Bersin, J. - The Modern Learning Ecosystem

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology has transformed training delivery from classroom-only to a rich ecosystem of modalities. The educational technologist must select the right modality for each learning objective, audience, and organizational constraint.
key_concepts: Synchronous virtual training: webinars, virtual classrooms, breakout activities
Asynchronous e-learning: self-paced modules, video-based learning
Blended training: combining modalities for optimal results
Microlearning for reinforcement and performance support
Platforms: LMS (Cornerstone, Docebo), LXP (Degreed, EdCast), authoring (Articulate, Captivate)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Modality selection exercise: given five different training objectives with different constraints (time, budget, geography, compliance requirement), select and justify the optimal delivery modality for each

review.value: LMS manages formal training: assign, track, report. LXP enables informal learning: curate, recommend, share. Many organizations are moving from compliance-driven LMS to engagement-driven LXP.
The most effective corporate training programs blend modalities: asynchronous pre-work, synchronous skill practice, on-the-job application, spaced microlearning reinforcement.

learning_objectives: Apply Kirkpatrick's four levels to evaluate training programs
Calculate training ROI using Phillips' Level 5 methodology
Design evaluation instruments for each level
readings: Kirkpatrick, J. & Kirkpatrick, W. - Kirkpatrick's Four Levels of Training Evaluation
Phillips, J. - Return on Investment in Training and Performance Improvement Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: In corporate contexts, training must demonstrate value to the business. Kirkpatrick's model provides the framework, and Phillips' ROI methodology provides the financial argument. Together, they equip the training professional to defend budgets and prove impact.
key_concepts: Level 1 Reaction: did participants value the training?
Level 2 Learning: did knowledge and skills improve?
Level 3 Behavior: are participants applying new skills on the job?
Level 4 Results: did business metrics improve?
Level 5 ROI: do the benefits exceed the costs?
lecture_notes.demonstration: ROI calculation exercise: given training costs and pre/post performance data, calculate the ROI of a sales training program and present the business case to management

review.value: ROI = ((Program Benefits - Program Costs) / Program Costs) × 100. An ROI of 150% means the program returned $1.50 for every $1 invested.
The biggest drop-off in evaluation: many organizations measure Levels 1 and 2 but not Level 3 (on-the-job application). Without Level 3 data, you cannot attribute business results to training.

learning_objectives: Design effective professional development programs for educators
Apply evidence-based PD principles: sustained, collaborative, content-focused
Evaluate common PD models: coaching, PLCs, workshops, online courses
readings: Desimone, L. - Improving Impact Studies of Teachers' Professional Development
Darling-Hammond, L. et al. - Effective Teacher Professional Development (Learning Policy Institute)

lecture_notes.introduction: Teacher professional development is one of the most heavily invested and poorly executed training domains in education. Research is clear about what works, yet most PD continues to use one-shot workshop models that have no measurable impact on teaching practice.
key_concepts: What works: sustained (20+ hours), collaborative, content-focused, active learning, coherent
What does not work: one-shot workshops, sit-and-get, disconnected from daily practice
Coaching: individualized, job-embedded support with modeling, observation, and feedback
Professional Learning Communities: structured collaborative inquiry
Technology-enabled PD: online communities, video-based coaching, micro-credentialing
lecture_notes.demonstration: PD program design: design a year-long professional development program for teachers integrating a new educational technology, applying all five research-based effectiveness criteria
setup_requirements: Case studies of effective and ineffective PD programs

review.value: Five evidence-based features: content focus, active learning, coherence with school goals, sustained duration, and collective participation. PD lacking any of these features shows minimal impact.
Studies show that PD with embedded coaching transfers 90% of new skills to classroom practice, compared to 10% for traditional workshops without follow-up.

class_schedule.topic: Emerging Trends: AI-Powered Training and Skills-Based Organizations
learning_objectives: Evaluate AI-powered personalization in corporate training
Describe the shift from role-based to skills-based workforce development
Design training programs for emerging workforce trends: remote work, gig economy, continuous reskilling
readings: World Economic Forum - The Future of Jobs Report (current year)
Bersin, J. - The Definitive Guide to Learning Ecosystems (excerpts)

lecture_notes.introduction: The training landscape is undergoing fundamental transformation. AI enables hyper-personalized learning paths. Skills-based hiring is replacing degree-based hiring. Remote work demands new training delivery models. The educational technologist who understands these trends will be indispensable.
key_concepts: AI in training: adaptive paths, content generation, chatbot coaching, skills inference
Skills taxonomies and ontologies: mapping organizational skills gaps
Skills-based organizations: hiring and developing for skills, not just degrees
Continuous reskilling: from training events to learning cultures
Micro-credentials and digital badges as portable skill verification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Skills gap analysis: using a skills taxonomy, map the current and required skills for a hypothetical role undergoing digital transformation, then design a learning pathway to close the gap

review.value: Deloitte reports that skills-based organizations are 63% more likely to achieve results. Mapping and developing skills — not just delivering courses — is the future of the training function.
Digital badges and micro-credentials provide verified, portable evidence of specific skills. They bridge formal education and workplace learning, and are increasingly accepted by employers.

questions.question: A company asks you to create a 4-hour compliance training e-learning module. Using adult learning principles, explain why this approach is problematic and propose an alternative design.
questions.answer: A 4-hour monolithic e-learning module violates multiple adult learning principles: it ignores adult learners' limited tolerance for irrelevant content (Knowles: relevance), removes autonomy by forcing a linear path (Knowles: self-direction), and provides no experiential application (Kolb). The content-dump format maximizes seat time but minimizes learning transfer. Alternative: break the content into 10-15 minute microlearning modules organized by topic. Start each module with a realistic workplace scenario that demonstrates relevance. Include interactive decision-making exercises instead of passive reading. Spread the modules over 2-3 weeks with spaced retrieval quizzes. Include a short final scenario-based assessment that measures application, not recall.

rubric.key: Identifies specific adult learning principle violations
Proposes concrete alternative design with structure
Alternative addresses relevance, autonomy, and application

questions.question: Explain the difference between an LMS and an LXP in corporate training contexts. When would you recommend each?
questions.answer: An LMS (Learning Management System) is organization-controlled: it assigns courses, tracks completions, and generates compliance reports. Best for: mandatory compliance training, structured onboarding programs, and regulated industries requiring documented training records. An LXP (Learning Experience Platform) is learner-controlled: it curates content from multiple sources, uses AI to recommend learning, and encourages social sharing. Best for: continuous professional development, self-directed skill building, and organizations wanting to build a learning culture rather than enforce training completion. Many organizations use both: LMS for compliance, LXP for development.

rubric.key: Accurate distinction between LMS and LXP

quizzes.topic: Evaluation, Faculty PD, and Emerging Trends

questions.question: Most organizations evaluate training only at Kirkpatrick Levels 1 and 2. Explain why this is insufficient and describe how you would measure Level 3 (Behavior) for a customer service training program.
questions.answer: Levels 1 (reaction: did they enjoy it?) and 2 (learning: did they pass the test?) only measure the training event itself, not whether it changes job behavior or business outcomes. Participants can enjoy training and pass a quiz but never apply the skills. Level 3 measures on-the-job behavior change, which is the actual goal of training. For customer service training: (1) Pre/post observation of customer interactions using a behavioral checklist. (2) Mystery shopper evaluations comparing pre-training and 30/60/90-day post-training scores. (3) Manager surveys rating employee application of new skills. (4) Analysis of customer satisfaction scores and first-call resolution rates. Measure at multiple time points (30, 60, 90 days) to assess whether behavior change persists.

rubric.key: Includes multiple time points for persistence

questions.question: Research by Darling-Hammond et al. identifies five features of effective teacher professional development. Name three and explain why one-shot workshops typically fail to include them.
questions.answer: Three features: (1) Sustained duration — effective PD involves 20+ hours of engagement over weeks or months, allowing practice, reflection, and iteration. (2) Active learning — teachers must practice new strategies, not just hear about them. (3) Collective participation — teachers from the same school or department learning together can support each other's implementation. One-shot workshops fail because they are too short (2-3 hours), use passive delivery (sit-and-get), isolate teachers from their colleagues' ongoing support, and have no follow-up mechanism to support implementation. Teachers return to their classrooms with enthusiasm that fades within days because there is no structure for sustained practice and feedback.

rubric.key: Explains why one-shot workshops miss these features

major_assignments.title: Professional Development Program Design
major_assignments.description: Design a comprehensive professional development or training program for a specific audience (corporate employees, K-12 teachers, healthcare professionals, or another professional group). The program must include needs analysis, learning objectives, a blended delivery design, assessment strategy, and a Kirkpatrick Level 3+ evaluation plan with ROI projection.
objectives: Apply adult learning principles to program design
Design a blended training delivery strategy
Create a multi-level evaluation plan with ROI projection
Produce a professional program proposal suitable for a real client
deliverables: Blended delivery plan with modality justification
Kirkpatrick evaluation plan with sample instruments

criteria: Identifies a real performance gap with evidence
Distinguishes training needs from non-training solutions
Content and activities promote transfer to practice

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on adult learning, training design, and evaluation

participation.description: In-class design exercises, role-plays, and peer critiques

midterm_examination.description: Covers adult learning, training delivery, and evaluation frameworks

final_project.description: Complete training program with prototype materials and evaluation instruments

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected. Design workshops and role-play exercises require participation. More than 3 absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Design milestones must be met for peer feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer feedback is integral. All program designs must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Cite all sources. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access. Access to an e-learning authoring tool (Articulate Rise trial or H5P) for prototype materials.
